text: The surgeon marks the bowel, selecting a safe margin before beginning the dissection.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to restore normal anatomy, initially planning to avoid high ligation of the bowel and focus on lateral mobilization.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization, revealing the ureter. As dissection continues around the adnexa, it becomes evident how closely it is adhered to the bowel, indicating the presence of a fistula.

The surgeon mobilizes the gonadal vessels in preparation for a left salpingo-oophorectomy. The adnexa appear fused to the bowel due to the fistula. White, rock-hard lymph nodes are visible in the region, prompting the decision for the salpingo-oophorectomy.
The surgeon has mobilized the pedicle and cleared the ureter, proceeding to divide the structures to gain better access to the left adherence to the sidewall.

The surgeon encounters difficulty with the dissection and enters the posterior retrorectal plane for better visualization. They proceed to dissect beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery over the sacral promontory, identify the ureter, and skeletonize the vessel before dividing it.

The surgeon continues mobilization in the retrorectal space, ensuring the ureter is visible and clear of the surgical field. The bowel is carefully swept free, allowing rapid dissection along the adherence without risk of ureteral injury.

The surgeon encounters unexpected adherence while dissecting, revealing a granulation-filled cavity that resembles a fistula or possibly a blind abscess cavity. The peritoneum is opened to improve visibility, confirming the ureter is safely positioned to the side, allowing dissection to proceed without risk of injury.
The surgeon examines a cavity near the ureter and bladder entry, confirming it is not as close as initially feared. The view provides clear access to the remaining attachments for dissection.

The surgeon successfully frees the sigmoid colon from the left pelvic sidewall. The rectum is mobilized downwards to separate it from the vagina, with the absence of a uterus noted. The plan for transvaginal extraction is aborted due to the narrow introitus.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection to gain length for the anastomosis, using sharp cautery dissection lower down and a vessel sealer higher up for better control and hemostasis. The mesentery is lifted off the kidney towards the flexure, following a visible white line. The proximal mesentery is divided at a chosen margin, extending several centimeters more proximal due to thickened diverticulum in the area.
The surgeon divides the rectal mesentery at a lower point than usual, near the rectosigmoid junction, clearing the mesentery from the posterior aspect of the bowel.

The surgeon clears fat from the anterior aspect of the surgical field. The camera is repositioned to provide a better view as a twelve-millimeter port is inserted through the rectus muscle. The proximal bowel is divided, followed by the division of the distal bowel.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis, suturing the posterior staple lines with two OPDS sutures. ICG imaging confirms enhancement of the inner lip inside the colotomy and proctotomy. The inner layer is sutured using a 3 OV lock, starting in the middle and running to the left, followed by a new suture from the middle to the right, and then across anteriorly.
The surgeon ties two sutures together at the top of the anastomosis, creating a circumferential two-layer closure with an additional anterior row imbricated using a two OPDS suture.
The surgeon ties the suture across to the original post, completing the anastomosis. A leak test of the bowel is then performed to ensure the integrity of the connection.

The surgeon addresses bleeding from the nephropoietic appendage and tests the rectum, confirming the anastomosis is widely patent with no bleeding or leaks. The surgical field is cleaned, and the bladder is tested with saline, revealing a fistula connection.
The surgeon observes no leak during the testing of the anastomosis. Despite the absence of a leak, the surgeon uses a remnant of 2-0 PDS suture to close the area, demonstrating the ease of closure.
